imports and config¶

In [1]:
from ollama import chat
from transformers import pipeline


import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv(".env")
Out[1]:
True

🤖 Agentic LLMs / LLM Agents / Agentic AI¶

Instead of a rigid binary definition, let’s think of Agents in terms of agency and autonomy.

  • Level 0: Agents with no tools (basic inference tasks).
  • Level 1: Agents with tools for autonomous task execution.
  • Level 2: Agents with knowledge, combining memory and reasoning.
  • Level 3: Teams of specialized agents collaborating on complex workflows.

Credit: https://docs.agno.com/agents/introduction

Anthropic's Take on Agents¶

  • Workflows are systems where LLMs and tools are orchestrated through predefined code paths.
  • Agents, on the other hand, are systems where LLMs dynamically direct their own processes and tool usage, maintaining control over how they accomplish tasks.

Augnmented LLM (a workflow)¶

Credit: https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-effective-agents

Parallel LLM Calls (again, a workflow)¶

Credit: https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-effective-agents

Orchestrator (still a workflow)¶

Credit: https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-effective-agents

Agent (finally!)¶

Credit: https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-effective-agents

🤖 Autoregressive factorization (Language Model)¶

Let the token sequence be $(w_0, w_1, \dots, w_5)$

We start from the joint distribution:

$$ p(\text{words}) = p(w_0, w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4, w_5) $$

Apply the chain rule once:

$$ p(w_0, w_1, \dots, w_5) = p(w_0)\, p(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_5 \mid w_0). $$

Apply the chain rule again to the conditional term:

$$ p(w_0, w_1, \dots, w_5) = p(w_0)\, p(w_1 \mid w_0)\, p(w_2, \dots, w_5 \mid w_0, w_1). $$

Repeat the same expansion step-by-step:

$$ \begin{aligned} p(w_0, w_1, \dots, w_5) &= p(w_0)\, p(w_1 \mid w_0)\, p(w_2 \mid w_0, w_1) \\ &\quad \times p(w_3 \mid w_0, w_1, w_2)\, p(w_4 \mid w_0, w_1, w_2, w_3) \\ &\quad \times p(w_5 \mid w_0, w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4). \end{aligned} $$

General form:

$$ \boxed{% p(w_0, w_1, \dots, w_N) = \prod_{t=0}^{N} p\bigl(w_t \mid w_{<t}\bigr) } $$

where $w_{<t} = (w_0, \dots, w_{t-1})$

For a token at index $i$:

$$ P\bigl(w_i \mid w_{<i}\bigr) $$

$$ P\bigl(\text{token}_i \mid \text{context}_{<i}\bigr) $$

In [3]:
generator = pipeline('text-generation', model="facebook/opt-125m")
response = generator("What is the captial of Himachal Pradesh?", max_length=100)

print("---")
print(response[0]["generated_text"])

Device set to use cuda:0
Truncation was not explicitly activated but `max_length` is provided a specific value, please use `truncation=True` to explicitly truncate examples to max length. Defaulting to 'longest_first' truncation strategy. If you encode pairs of sequences (GLUE-style) with the tokenizer you can select this strategy more precisely by providing a specific strategy to `truncation`.
Both `max_new_tokens` (=256) and `max_length`(=101) seem to have been set. `max_new_tokens` will take precedence. Please refer to the documentation for more information. (https://huggingface.co/docs/transformers/main/en/main_classes/text_generation)

---
What is the captial of Himachal Pradesh?

The Himachal Pradesh is one of the most beautiful places in the country. It is well known for its landscapes, beauty and people. The Himachal Pradesh is considered the state of India by many.
People have been visiting Himachal Pradesh for the last few years.
People of Himachal Pradesh have been visiting Himachal Pradesh for the last 7 years. From the years of time, people have been visiting Himachal Pradesh for the best experiences.
In the year 2011, Himachal Pradesh has been the happiest place in India. People of Himachal Pradesh have been living in the year 2011 and have been making the best time for the people living in the year 2011.
Himachal Pradesh is the most beautiful state in India. The state is rich in beauty and people have made the best time for the people living in the year 2011.
There are several places in Himachal Pradesh that have got very good popularity among the people.
In Himachal Pradesh, people are making the best time for the people living in the year 2011.
The people are making the best time for the people living in the year 2011.
Himachal Pradesh has all the things that people want in a state

---
max has 25 points, norris has 18 points. so the person with more points is going to get a better team and the person with less points wins. The reason people play ranked is because it is fun and rewarding.
I like ranked. It's just that when playing against other players, it's easy to get sucked into the match.  But I love ranked. It's not like I'm playing ranked because I'm "good at" it's like I'm playing ranked because I'm "good at" it's like I'm playing ranked because I'm "good at" it's like I'm playing ranked because I'm "good at" it's like I'm playing ranked because I'm "good at" it's like I'm playing ranked because I'm "good at" it's like I'm playing ranked because I'm "good at" it's like I'm playing ranked because I'm "good at" it's like I'm playing ranked because I'm "good at" it's like I'm playing ranked because I'm "good at" it's like I'm playing ranked because I'm "good at" it's like I'm playing ranked because I'm "good at" it's like I'm playing ranked because I'm "good at" it's like I'm playing ranked because I'm "good at" it

Credit: https://openai.com/index/instruction-following/

In [5]:
response = chat(
    model='llama3.2', 
    messages=[
        {
            'role': 'system',
            'content': 'You are a helpful assistant. Give succint, and concise one sentence answers. Always respond in hindi.',
        },
        {
            'role': 'user',
            'content': 'What is the captial of Himachal Pradesh?',
        },
    ]
)
print("---")
print(response.message.content)

---
शिमला हिमाचल प्रदेश की राजधानी है।
In [6]:
response = chat(
    model='llama3.2', 
    messages=[
        {
            'role': 'user',
            'content': 'How many r are there in kookaburra?',
        },
    ]
)
print("---")
print(response.message.content)

---
There is only one "r" in the word "kookaburra".

💡 Chain of Thought¶

A series of intermediate reasoning steps.

Credit: https://arxiv.org/abs/2201.11903

In [ ]:
response = chat(
    model='llama3.2', 
    messages=[
        {
            'role': 'user',
            'content': 'How many r are there in kookaburra? Go through each letter and check if it is r or not.' + context,
        },
    ]
)
print("---")
print(response.message.content)

---
Let's go through the word "kookaburra" to count the number of R's:

1. K - No R
2. O - No R
3. O - No R
4. K - No R
5. A - No R
6. B - No R
7. U - No R
8. R - Yes!
9. R - Yes!

There are 2 R's in the word "kookaburra".

🧑‍💻 Reasoning LLMs¶

Credit: https://newsletter.maartengrootendorst.com/p/a-visual-guide-to-reasoning-llms

In [6]:
# we dont need CoT anymore, the LLMs can reason and show you their latent thoughts!
response = chat(
    model='deepseek-r1:8b', 
    messages=[
        {
            'role': 'user',
            'content': 'What is 1 + 1?',
        },
    ]
)
print("---")
print("Thinking:", response.message.model_dump().get('thinking', 'No thinking available'))
print("===")
print("Response:", response.message.content)

---
Thinking: First, the user asked: "What is 1 + 1?" This is a very basic arithmetic question. I know that 1 + 1 equals 2. It's one of the simplest math facts.

I should respond clearly and directly. Since this is an AI conversation, I can be helpful and engaging. Maybe I can add a bit of humor or a light-hearted comment to make it more interesting, but I need to keep it simple.

Possible responses:

- Straightforward: "1 + 1 equals 2."

- With explanation: "Adding one to one gives you two."

- Humorous: "Well, if 1 + 1 was anything else, what would it be? But in standard arithmetic, it's 2."

But I should avoid being too complicated for such a simple question. The user might be testing me or just starting a conversation.

Also, consider the context. This could be a trick question or something in a different base, but the user didn't specify, so I should assume base 10.

In binary, 1 + 1 is 10, but that might be overcomplicating it. The question doesn't indicate any special context, so I'll go with the standard decimal answer.

Finally, structure the response: Start with the answer, then perhaps a brief explanation or acknowledgment.

Response: "1 + 1 equals 2. That's a basic math fact!"

Or: "The sum of 1 and 1 is 2."

I think that's sufficient.

===
Response: 1 + 1 equals 2. That's a fundamental concept in arithmetic! If you have any more questions, feel free to ask. 😊

😔 Limitations of (Isolated) LLMs¶

😵‍💫 Hallucination: Generation of incorrect information with high confidence¶

🤨 Knowledge cutoff: Limited to training data timeframe¶

🥸 Lack of attribution: No direct source citations¶

🔒 Data privacy: Limited to public training data, no access to proprietary information¶

↔️ Limited context length: Constraints due to attention mechanism¶

💡 RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation)¶

Credit: https://aws.amazon.com/what-is/retrieval-augmented-generation

In [7]:
from agno.agent import Agent
from agno.knowledge.embedder.sentence_transformer import SentenceTransformerEmbedder
from agno.knowledge.knowledge import Knowledge
from agno.models.ollama import Ollama
from agno.vectordb.lancedb import LanceDb, SearchType

# Create knowledge base with hybrid search
knowledge = Knowledge(
    vector_db=LanceDb(
        uri="./lancedb",
        table_name="nvidia_8k_document",
        search_type=SearchType.keyword,  # Semantic + keyword search
        embedder=SentenceTransformerEmbedder()
    ),
)

# knowledge.add_content(
#     name="nvidia_8k_document",
#     path="./nvidia-8k.txt"
# )

# Create agent with knowledge and session persistence
agno_assist = Agent(
    name="Agno Assist",
    model=Ollama(id="llama3.1:8b"),
    knowledge=knowledge,
    markdown=True,
)

response = agno_assist.run("Explain Project GR00T AI? Look into your knowledge base for the answer. Answer in concise manner.")
print(response.content)

INFO Found 3 documents                                                                                             

Project GR00T AI is a project by NVIDIA that aims to develop humanoid robots with advanced artificial intelligence capabilities. The project involves the use of simulation tools for robot learning and development, as well as generative AI tools and perception workflows for robotics developers. The goal of Project GR00T AI is to enable the creation of robots that can perform complex tasks and interact with their environment in a more human-like way.

🛠️ Tool Use¶

In [29]:
from agno.agent import Agent
from agno.models.ollama import Ollama
from agno.tools.duckduckgo import DuckDuckGoTools

# Create agent with DuckDuckGo search tool
agno_assist = Agent(
    name="Agno Assist",
    model=Ollama(id="llama3.1:8b"),
    tools=[DuckDuckGoTools()],
    markdown=True,
)

response = agno_assist.run("Explain Project GR00T AI? Search the web for the answer. Answer in concise manner.")
print(response.content)

**Project GR00T AI**

Project GR00T AI, also known as NVIDIA Isaac GR00T, is a research initiative and development platform by NVIDIA. It aims to develop general-purpose robot foundation models and data pipelines for accelerating humanoid robotics research and development.

**Key Features:**

* Generalist robot foundation models
* Data pipelines for accelerating humanoid robotics research and development

**Purpose:** To advance the field of humanoid robotics and enable faster development of robots with human-like capabilities.

🧠 Memory¶

In [8]:
from agno.agent import Agent
from agno.models.ollama import Ollama
from agno.tools.mem0 import Mem0Tools

user_id = "agno"

# Create agent with Mem0Tools for memory management
agent = Agent(
    model=Ollama(id="llama3.1:8b"),
    tools=[Mem0Tools(user_id=user_id)],
    instructions=[
        "You are my calendar assistant.",
        "You have to keep track of my schedule.",
        "Store important information about my events and preferences.",
        "When I ask you to check my schedule for a specific date and time, search your memory for any events scheduled for that date and time.",
        "If I have an event scheduled, you should tell me that I have an event scheduled for that date and time.",
        "If I don't have an event scheduled, you should tell me that my schedule is free for that date and time.",
    ],
    markdown=True,
)

# First, let's add some memories
agent.print_response("Remember that my name is Puneet and I live in Chandigarh.", stream=True)
agent.print_response("Remember that I'm going to a concert on April 8 from 6 - 9 PM.", stream=True)

# Now check if schedule is free
agent.print_response("I want to go to the movies on April 8 around 6:30 PM, is my schedule free?", stream=True)

# https://app.mem0.ai/dashboard/memories

Output()












Output()
























Output()

So many cool tools, but no standard way to use them!¶






















🌐 Model Context Protocol¶




Credit: https://modelcontextprotocol.io/








Credit: https://www.atlassian.com/blog/announcements/remote-mcp-server








Credit: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/ai-agents-and-the-model-context-protocol-ugcPost-7330644107356217345-Czgu/




















In [1]:






from textwrap import dedent
import asyncio
import nest_asyncio
from agno.agent import Agent
from agno.models.ollama import Ollama
from agno.tools.mcp import MCPTools

# Allow nested event loops (needed for Jupyter notebooks)
nest_asyncio.apply()


async def run_agent(message: str) -> None:
    """Run the filesystem agent with the given message."""

    file_path = "/home/abrol/agents/"

    # Initialize and connect to the MCP server to access the filesystem
    print("calling", f"npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem {file_path}")
    mcp_tools = MCPTools(command=f"npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem {file_path}")
    await mcp_tools.connect()

    try:
        agent = Agent(
            model=Ollama(id="llama3.1:8b"),
            tools=[mcp_tools],
            instructions=dedent("""\
                You are a filesystem assistant. Help users explore files and directories.

                - Navigate the filesystem to answer questions
                - Use the read_file tool to read the contents of files\
            """),
            markdown=True,
        )

        # Run the agent
        await agent.aprint_response(message, stream=True)
    finally:
        # Always close the connection when done
        await mcp_tools.close()


# Example usage - run directly with await in Jupyter
# Basic example - exploring project license
await run_agent("What is the license for /home/abrol/agents/? Read the LICENSE.txt file to find out.")

























calling npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem /home/abrol/agents/













Output()
